src="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2024/01/18/multimedia/18db-davos-clvm/18db-davos-clvm-articleLarge.jpg?quality=75&amp;auto=webp&amp;disable=upscale" 600px) and (max-width: 1004px)) 84vw, (min-width: 1005px) 60vw, 100vw" decoding="async" width="600" height="400"/></picture></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-caption" class="css-gbc9ki ewdxa0s0"><span class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Critics say there is relatively little discussion about antisemitism at this year’s World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Denis Balibouse/Reuters</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><h2 class="css-9ycfei eoo0vm40" id="link-43e32d69">A topic that’s not getting a lot of attention </h2><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Attendees at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, have been speaking at length about artificial intelligence, the war in Ukraine, the U.S. election, climate change and more.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">But one topic dominating a lot of discussion among U.S. business leaders and policymakers has been largely missing from the official agenda: rising antisemitism after the Oct. 7 Hamas-led attacks on Israel. Some executives are working to change that.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Only one panel to discuss the topic is on the agenda </strong>out of the forum’s hundreds of presentations.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">The panelists at the <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.weforum.org/events/world-economic-forum-annual-meeting-2024/sessions/addressing-antisemitism" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">session</a> this afternoon include Doug Emhoff, the husband of Vice President Kamala Harris; Michal Herzog, the wife of Israel’s president; and Jonathan Greenblatt of the Anti-Defamation League. (Isaac Herzog, Israel’s president, spoke this morning.)</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">“I’m disappointed that there isn’t more of a conversation about antisemitism here,”</strong> Leon Kalvaria, the chairman of Citigroup’s Institutional Clients Group and a longtime Davos attendee, told DealBook. “I hope to hear it brought up more as the week progresses because leaders in both the private and public sectors have a real responsibility not only to make it clear that this kind of intolerance is unacceptable, but to also discuss the very real impact it has around the world.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Other executives are raising the issue privately.</strong> On Tuesday, there was a private screening of a 47-minute film, put together by the Israeli military, of the Oct. 7 attacks, which the military says was drawn largely from footage shot by the assailants.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Davos typically is a conference filled with hope, but this terrible footage left the audience in shambles,” Greenblatt told DealBook. “People walked out of the room in silence, either crying or simply shellshocked.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Palantir held an event on Wednesday featuring relatives of the Israelis held hostage since the Oct. 7 attacks. </strong>The “off the record” session was hosted by Alex Karp, the data consulting company’s C.E.O., but <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-01-18/davos-2024-palantir-hosted-israel-talk-draws-jassy-benioff-dell" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Bloomberg</a> and <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://jewishinsider.com/2024/01/palantir-convenes-high-level-gathering-in-davos-to-support-israeli-hostages/" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Jewish Insider</a> quickly reported on it. Among the executives who reportedly attended were Andy Jassy of Amazon; the tech mogul Michael Dell; Martin Sorrell, the advertising executive; Ruth Porat, the C.F.O. of Alphabet; and Nicola Mendelsohn, head of Meta’s global business group.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Antisemitism as a kind of prejudice has always been the canary in the coal mine for your society isn’t working,” Karp told Andrew on CNBC.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Davos has a long history of avoiding sensitive subjects,</strong> seeking to appease countries and businesses that don’t want to be drawn into political debates. One example is L.G.B.T.Q. issues, which stayed off the forum’s agenda for years. </p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Privately, C.E.O.s are worried, </strong>according to Greenblatt. “I am hearing from C.E.O.s on the sidelines. They want to talk about antisemitism,” he told DealBook.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">He added that some executives said that while other minority groups received outpourings of support after the murder of George Floyd and anti-Asian American violence, the phones of Jewish executives and colleagues didn’t ring. “And frankly, they’re still not ringing,” he said.</p><h3 class="css-vgpz0b e1gnsphs0" id="link-594cd70c"><span>HERE’S WHAT’S HAPPENING </span></h3><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Apple will remove a feature from its watches after losing a patent fight.</strong> The tech giant’s Apple Watch Series 9 and Watch Ultra 2 will be sold without <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/17/technology/apple-watch-oximetry-international-trade-commission.html" title="">a functioning blood-oxygen sensor from today</a>. The decision comes after an International Trade Commission ruling that Apple violated patents held by Masimo, a medical technology company. A court rejected Apple’s bid to delay a sales ban until a ruling is issued on its appeal of the commission’s decision.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">TSMC predicts sharp growth in 2024 on booming demand for A.I. chips.</strong> The world’s largest contract chipmaker said revenues would <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.ft.com/content/2126b014-943d-4c03-8c8b-2dd07b4bbb74" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">grow as much as 25 percent</a> this year as companies spend heavily on artificial intelligence. The Taiwanese company also warned that it expected delays in <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.wsj.com/tech/chip-giant-tsmc-foresees-delay-at-second-arizona-plant-22fe1e41?mod=hp_lead_pos5" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">building its second production plant in Arizona</a> and cast doubts on plans to produce its most advanced chips there.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Sheryl Sandberg is leaving Meta’s board.</strong> The longtime executive and close adviser to Mark Zuckerberg, Meta’s founder, stepped down as C.O.O. in 2022, after a string of controversies around the social media giant’s handling of disinformation and hate speech on its platforms. Sandberg said she <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/17/technology/sheryl-sandberg-meta-board.html" title="">would remain an</a> adviser to the company.</p><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-medium css-d754w4 e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0" data-testid="photoviewer-children-figure"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:9.022222222222224px"></div></div></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-caption" class="css-gbc9ki ewdxa0s0"></figcaption></figure></div></div><h2 class="css-9ycfei eoo0vm40" id="link-6e9de3d4">Higher for how much longer? </h2><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Investors hoping for a Davos bounce are probably feeling disappointed.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Stocks have been on a roller coaster in recent days and a host of Wall Street C.E.O.s as well as Christine Lagarde, the president of the European Central Bank, are sending a message to investors from the Swiss Alps: Don’t get out over your skis and assume that interest rate cuts are imminent.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Traders </strong><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10"><em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">are</em></strong><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10"> winding back their bets on a Fed move.</strong> The odds of a March rate cut are <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/interest-rates/cme-fedwatch-tool.html" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">61 percent</a>, down from a more confident 96 percent at the start of January. But the more tempered outlook is still too optimistic, Ron O’Hanley, State Street’s C.E.O., <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-01-17/bankers-at-davos-add-to-chorus-of-caution-on-interest-rate-cuts?srnd=undefined" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">told Bloomberg</a>. “The Fed was very clear in their dot plot,” he said, “and I don’t know why the markets decided to double it and then go to town on that.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">The markets rallied at the end of 2023 and the S&amp;P 500 flirted with a record high. </strong>The surge was fueled by<strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10"> </strong>hopes that central bankers were done raising rates, and that a new phase of lower borrowing costs was close. But central bankers have been urging caution ever since Jay Powell, the Fed chair, signaled last month that three rate cuts were likely in 2024.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Lagarde delivered her latest warning at Davos </strong>on Wednesday, saying that investors’ bullish bets are potentially harmful. “It is not helping our fight against inflation,” she said, helping to set off a <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.ft.com/content/3650273d-458e-4e02-929e-bbb3ac7c3a40" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">sell-off in stocks and bonds</a>.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Mixed messages on the U.S. economy are adding to the confusion.</strong> <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.cnbc.com/2024/01/17/retail-sales-december-2023.html" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Consumers are still spending</a>, according to the latest retail sales data, and that could hamper the Fed’s efforts to lower inflation. At the same time, the labor market is beginning to slow in some parts of the country, the central bank’s <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-01-17/fed-s-beige-book-shows-strong-consumer-cooling-labor-market" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">“beige book” survey</a>, which was published on Wednesday, showed. That could have the opposite effect on prices.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Some on Wall Street see inflation as having peaked.</strong> Analysts at Morgan Stanley, UBS and Barclays forecast that core inflation, which strips out food and fuel, will fall more this year. This week, Barclays <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/markets-wont-give-up-march-fed-cut-mike-dolan-2024-01-17/" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">moved its call</a> for the first rate cut to March from June.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Up next:</strong> Lagarde is set to speak again today, as is Raphael Bostic, the president of the Atlanta Fed. Bostic <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/12/20/business/dealbook/washington-us-steel-national-security.html" title="">made headlines</a> last month when he said that inflation was still too high and that he saw no “urgency” to lower borrowing costs, just days after Powell said the equivalent of three rate cuts were on the table this year.</p><hr class="css-7ad88g e1mu4ftr0"/><h2 class="css-9ycfei eoo0vm40" id="link-595824c7">Agencies brace for a blow </h2><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Supreme Court justices on Wednesday heard arguments in two cases involving commercial fishermen that could limit how federal agencies regulate business. The upshot: The court’s conservative majority seemed <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/17/us/supreme-court-chevron-case.html" title="">sympathetic to the argument</a> that changes to Washington’s regulatory power structure are needed.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">The cases represent a major test of the so-called Chevron deference.</strong> The decades-old doctrine has become a bedrock of administrative law. It stipulates that agencies are the ones to interpret statutes in their areas of expertise, and that the courts have little power to challenge them. After all, “agencies know things that courts do not,” Justice Elena Kagan said, adding, “that’s the basis of Chevron.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Kagan said that striking down the principle would be disruptive, because 70 Supreme Court decisions, and thousands more in the lower courts, rely on it.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Critics say that Chevron gives agencies too much power.</strong> Justice Neil Gorsuch contends that the Chevron deference <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://twitter.com/JCNSeverino/status/1747680875976823048" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">ends up harming the vulnerable</a>, citing “the immigrant, the veteran seeking his benefits, the social security disability applicant, who have no power to influence agencies.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">That said, powerful conservative voices, including the billionaire Charles Koch, want to see the doctrine gutted. And <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.uschamber.com/assets/documents/U.S.-Chamber-Amicus-Brief-Loper-Bright-Enterpises-v.-Raimondo-U.S.-Supreme-Court.pdf" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">the Chamber of Commerce argued</a> in an amicus brief that the doctrine had contributed to agency overreach and an explosion of rules that impose “astronomical costs” on business “reaching as high as $1.9 trillion per year.”</p><hr class="css-7ad88g e1mu4ftr0"/></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><h2 class="css-9ycfei eoo0vm40" id="link-2ef6cdbf">“If she doesn’t get traction in New Hampshire, you don’t throw money down a rat hole.” </h2><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">— </em><a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.ft.com/content/4c474184-3806-4d7c-8c46-6090901c5253" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank"><em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">Ken Langone</em></a><em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">, the co-founder of Home Depot and a prominent backer of Nikki Haley, on whether he would give more money to her campaign heading into the Republican primary in New Hampshire next week.</em></p><hr class="css-7ad88g e1mu4ftr0"/><h2 class="css-9ycfei eoo0vm40" id="link-65347984">Amazon bets on local sports </h2><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Amazon said on Wednesday that it would buy a minority stake in the country’s largest operator of regional sports networks, ramping up a wider competition by Big Tech to broadcast live games from some of the biggest professional leagues.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">The tech giant will invest $115 million in Diamond Sports Group,</strong> which is bankrupt and which broadcasts about a third of all Major League Baseball, N.B.A. and N.H.L. games in some of the country’s biggest media markets under the banner of Bally Sports. Amazon will obtain the rights to air the games of more than 40 teams.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Amazon moved into live sports in 2017,</strong> when it acquired the rights to the N.F.L.’s Thursday night games. It has since expanded into soccer in Europe and North America. Apple, YouTube and Meta have also spent billions to stream sports, including N.F.L. games, soccer and tennis.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Diamond’s problems were closely monitored by the sports media world.</strong> Sinclair Broadcast Group acquired the company from Disney for $10.6 billion in 2019. But the coronavirus pandemic and a wider consumer shift to streaming platforms hammered <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/29/sports/regional-sports-networks-bankruptcy.html" title="">regional sports networks</a>.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">The company filed for bankruptcy last March</strong>, squeezed by a $9 billion debt load. That led some teams to try to move their games to free-to-air-channels. Others, like the Phoenix Suns and Utah Jazz, effectively tried to become <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.espn.com/nba/story/_/id/38496656/inside-utah-jazz-bold-new-tv-plan" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">broadcasters</a> by streaming games on their own platforms.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Amazon’s push into local markets may be aimed at moving beyond its typical demographic</strong>, with some analysts saying growth in Amazon Prime <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://therobinreport.com/amazon-prime-is-past-its-prime/#:~:text=However,%20Amazon%20Prime's%20domestic%20subscriber,on%20CIRP's%20most%20recent%20estimates." title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">is at risk of stalling</a>. Even if deals for regional sports teams reach only relatively few consumers, the company might still consider it a win if it drives more people to sign up to Prime.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">The deal will need to be approved by a bankruptcy judge as part of Diamond’s restructuring agreement.</p><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-medium css-d754w4 e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0" data-testid="photoviewer-children-figure"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:9.022222222222224px"></div></div></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-caption" class="css-gbc9ki ewdxa0s0"></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><h3 class="css-vgpz0b e1gnsphs0" id="link-7c091e00"><span>THE SPEED READ </span></h3><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Deals</strong></p><ul class="css-1le37cb ez3869y0"><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">Bobby Jain, the former Credit Suisse deal maker, reportedly aims to <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.ft.com/content/6762785a-202f-4e2e-acae-1a5e44a6fd06" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">launch a new hedge fund</a> in July with $5 billion to $6 billion in assets, falling short of his fund-raising target. (FT)</p></li><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">Citigroup’s former chief economist for China is among those who will <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-01-18/citigroup-s-ex-china-chief-economist-exiting-as-bank-cuts-jobs?srnd=markets-vp" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">reportedly leave the bank</a> amid a wider restructuring that is expected to result in thousands of job cuts. (Bloomberg)</p></li><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">Amancio Ortega, a founder of the Spanish fast-fashion retailer Zara, is <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.ft.com/content/ac7e5b0e-bde3-4d1b-8cd2-d89918e4c4b8" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">planning to cash in on the property downturn</a> by expanding his commercial real estate holdings. (FT)</p></li></ul><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Policy</strong></p><ul class="css-1le37cb ez3869y0"><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">“Dean Phillips, Bill Ackman, <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/17/us/politics/dean-phillips-bruce-ackman-dei.html" title="">a $1 Million Gift and a Website Tweak</a>” (NYT)</p></li><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">Ron DeSantis’s <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.politico.com/news/2024/01/17/desantis-new-hampshire-south-carolina-strategy-00136239" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">New Hampshire primary strategy</a> involves largely skipping the state. (Politico)</p></li></ul><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Best of the rest</strong></p><ul class="css-1le37cb ez3869y0"><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">“Making <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2024-01-17/making-esg-a-crime?srnd=opinion" title="" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">E.S.G. a Crime</a>” (Bloomberg Opinion)</p></li><li class="css-1i3ul0c eoqvrfo0"><p class="css-1il0jfh evys1bk0">Why is <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/01/18/world/europe/german-economy-standstill.html" title="">Germany</a>, once a powerhouse, in an economic rut? (NYT)</p></li></ul><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-medium css-d754w4 e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0" data-testid="photoviewer-children-figure"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:9.022222222222224px"></div></div></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-caption" class="css-gbc9ki ewdxa0s0"></figcaption></figure></div></div><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">We’d like your feedback! Please email thoughts and suggestions to <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/mailto:dealbook@nytimes.com" title="">dealbook@nytimes.com</a>.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div></section><div class="bottom-of-article"><div class="css-ddxw8g"></div><div class="css-1jp38cr"><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/andrew-ross-sorkin">Andrew Ross Sorkin</a></span> is a columnist and the founder and editor at large of DealBook. He is a co-anchor of CNBC’s "Squawk Box" and the author of &#8220;Too Big to Fail.&#8221; He is also a co-creator of the Showtime drama series "Billions."<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/andrew-ross-sorkin">More about Andrew Ross Sorkin</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/ravi-mattu">Ravi Mattu</a></span> is the managing editor of DealBook, based in London. He joined The New York Times in 2022 from the Financial Times, where he held a number of senior roles in Hong Kong and London.<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/ravi-mattu">More about Ravi Mattu</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/bernhard-warner">Bernhard Warner</a></span> is a senior editor for DealBook, a newsletter from The Times, covering business trends, the economy and the markets.<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/bernhard-warner">More about Bernhard Warner</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/sarah-kessler">Sarah Kessler</a></span> is an editor for the DealBook newsletter and writes features on business and how workplaces are changing.<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/sarah-kessler">More about Sarah Kessler</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p>Michael de la Merced joined The Times as a reporter in 2006, covering Wall Street and finance. Among his main coverage areas are mergers and acquisitions, bankruptcies and the private equity industry.<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/michael-j-de-la-merced">More about Michael J. de la Merced</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/lauren-hirsch">Lauren Hirsch</a></span> joined The Times from CNBC in 2020, covering deals and the biggest stories on Wall Street.<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/lauren-hirsch">More about Lauren Hirsch</a></span></p></div></div><div class="css-cw8msf eqi4ubu0"><div class="css-kzd6pg"><p><span class="css-97bxx6"><a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/ephrat-livni">Ephrat Livni</a></span> reports from Washington on the intersection of business and policy for DealBook. Previously, she was a senior reporter at Quartz, covering law and politics, and has practiced law in the public and private sectors.&#160;&#160;<span class="css-kzd6pg"> <a class="authorPageLinkClass overrideLinkStyles" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240118134025/https://www.nytimes.com/by/ephrat-livni">More about Ephrat Livni</a></span></p></div></div></div><div role="toolbar" hall.","__typename":"Image"}},{"__typename":"Heading2Block","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"A topic that’s not getting a lot of attention ","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Attendees at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, have been speaking at length about artificial intelligence, the war in Ukraine, the U.S. election, climate change and more.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":0,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"But one topic dominating a lot of discussion among U.S. business leaders and policymakers has been largely missing from the official agenda: rising antisemitism after the Oct. 7 Hamas-led attacks on Israel. Some executives are working to change that.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":1,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Only one panel to discuss the topic is on the agenda ","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"out of the forum’s hundreds of presentations.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The panelists at the ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"session","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.weforum.org\u002Fevents\u002Fworld-economic-forum-annual-meeting-2024\u002Fsessions\u002Faddressing-antisemitism","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" this afternoon include Doug Emhoff, the husband of Vice President Kamala Harris; Michal Herzog, the wife of Israel’s president; and Jonathan Greenblatt of the Anti-Defamation League. (Isaac Herzog, Israel’s president, spoke this morning.)","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":2,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“I’m disappointed that there isn’t more of a conversation about antisemitism here,”","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" Leon Kalvaria, the chairman of Citigroup’s Institutional Clients Group and a longtime Davos attendee, told DealBook. “I hope to hear it brought up more as the week progresses because leaders in both the private and public sectors have a real responsibility not only to make it clear that this kind of intolerance is unacceptable, but to also discuss the very real impact it has around the world.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":true,"index":3,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Other executives are raising the issue privately.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" On Tuesday, there was a private screening of a 47-minute film, put together by the Israeli military, of the Oct. 7 attacks, which the military says was drawn largely from footage shot by the assailants.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":4,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“Davos typically is a conference filled with hope, but this terrible footage left the audience in shambles,” Greenblatt told DealBook. “People walked out of the room in silence, either crying or simply They want to talk about antisemitism,” he told DealBook.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":9,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"He added that some executives said that while other minority groups received outpourings of support after the murder of George Floyd and anti-Asian American violence, the phones of Jewish executives and colleagues didn’t ring. “And frankly, they’re still not ringing,” he said.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":10,"bad":false},{"__typename":"Heading3Block","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"HERE’S WHAT’S HAPPENING ","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Apple will remove a feature from its watches after losing a patent fight.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" The tech giant’s Apple Watch Series 9 and Watch Ultra 2 will be sold without ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"a functioning blood-oxygen sensor from today","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2024\u002F01\u002F17\u002Ftechnology\u002Fapple-watch-oximetry-international-trade-commission.html","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":". ","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Investors hoping for a Davos bounce are probably feeling disappointed.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Stocks have been on a roller coaster in recent days and a host of Wall Street C.E.O.s as well as Christine Lagarde, the president of the European Central Bank, are sending a message to investors from the Swiss Alps: Don’t get out over your skis and assume that interest rate cuts are imminent.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":true,"index":15,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Traders ","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"are","formats":[{"__typename":"ItalicFormat","type":null},{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" winding back their bets on a Fed move.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" The odds of a March rate cut are ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"61 percent","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.cmegroup.com\u002Fmarkets\u002Finterest-rates\u002Fcme-fedwatch-tool.html","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", down from a more confident 96 percent at the start of January. But the more tempered outlook is still too optimistic, Ron O’Hanley, State Street’s C.E.O., ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"told Bloomberg","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.bloomberg.com\u002Fnews\u002Farticles\u002F2024-01-17\u002Fbankers-at-davos-add-to-chorus-of-caution-on-interest-rate-cuts?srnd=undefined","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":". “The Fed was very clear in their dot plot,” he said, “and I don’t know why the markets decided to double it and then go to town on that.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":16,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The markets rallied at the end of 2023 and the S&P 500 flirted with a record high. ","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The surge was fueled by","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" ","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"hopes that central bankers were done raising rates, and that a new phase of lower borrowing costs was close. But central bankers have been urging caution ever since Jay Powell, the Fed chair, signaled last month that three rate cuts were likely in 2024.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":17,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Lagarde delivered her latest warning at Davos ","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"on Wednesday, saying that investors’ bullish bets are potentially harmful. “It is not helping our fight against inflation,” she said, helping to set off a ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"sell-off in stocks and bonds","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.ft.com\u002Fcontent\u002F3650273d-458e-4e02-929e-bbb3ac7c3a40","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":".","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":18,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Mixed messages on the U.S. economy are adding to the confusion.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Consumers are still spending","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.cnbc.com\u002F2024\u002F01\u002F17\u002Fretail-sales-december-2023.html","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", according to the latest retail sales data, and that could hamper the Fed’s efforts to lower inflation. At the same time, the labor market is beginning to slow in some parts of the country, the central bank’s ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“beige book” survey","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.bloomberg.com\u002Fnews\u002Farticles\u002F2024-01-17\u002Ffed-s-beige-book-shows-strong-consumer-cooling-labor-market","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", which was published on Wednesday, showed. This week, Barclays ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"moved its call","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.reuters.com\u002Fmarkets\u002Fus\u002Fmarkets-wont-give-up-march-fed-cut-mike-dolan-2024-01-17\u002F","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" for the first rate cut to March from June.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":20,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Up next:","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" Lagarde is set to speak again today, as is Raphael Bostic, the president of the Atlanta Fed. Bostic ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"made headlines","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2023\u002F12\u002F20\u002Fbusiness\u002Fdealbook\u002Fwashington-us-steel-national-security.html","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" last month when he said that inflation was still too high and that he saw no “urgency” to lower borrowing costs, just days after Powell said the equivalent of three rate cuts were on the table this year.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":21,"bad":false},{"__typename":"RuleBlock","type":null},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":true,"index":22,"bad":false},{"__typename":"Heading2Block","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Agencies brace for a blow ","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Supreme Court justices on Wednesday heard arguments in two cases involving commercial fishermen that could limit how federal agencies regulate business. The upshot: The court’s conservative majority seemed ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"sympathetic to the argument","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2024\u002F01\u002F17\u002Fus\u002Fsupreme-court-chevron-case.html","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" that changes to Washington’s regulatory power structure are needed.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":23,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The cases represent a major test of the so-called Chevron deference.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" The decades-old doctrine has become a bedrock of administrative law. It stipulates that agencies are the ones to interpret statutes in their areas of expertise, and that the courts have little power to challenge them. After all, “agencies know things that courts do not,” Justice Elena Kagan said, adding, “that’s the basis of Chevron.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":24,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Kagan said that striking down the principle would be disruptive, because 70 Supreme Court decisions, and thousands more in the lower courts, rely on it.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":25,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Critics say that Chevron gives agencies too much power.","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" Justice Neil Gorsuch contends that the Chevron deference ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"ends up harming the vulnerable","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Ftwitter.com\u002FJCNSeverino\u002Fstatus\u002F1747680875976823048","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", citing “the immigrant, the veteran seeking his benefits, the social security disability applicant, who have no power to influence agencies.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":26,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"That said, powerful conservative voices, including the billionaire Charles Koch, want to see the doctrine gutted. And ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"the Chamber of Commerce argued","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240118134025\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.uschamber.com\u002Fassets\u002Fdocuments\u002FU.S.-Chamber-Amicus-Brief-Loper-Bright-Enterpises-v.-Raimondo-U.S.-Supreme-Court.pdf","title":"","uri":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" in an amicus brief that the doctrine had contributed to agency overreach and an explosion of rules that impose “astronomical costs” on business “reaching as high as $1.9 trillion per leagues.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":31,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"LEFT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The
